sentences.text: My name is Fania Wedro, F-A-N-I-A W-E-D-R-O.
Fania Elman, F-A-N-I-A E-L-M-A-N.
Your birth date and age, please.
Korzec.
K-O-R-Z-E-C.
Poland.
A very pretty, little town.
A very Zionistic town with about, close to, between 5,500 Jewish people.
So it was a very, very beautiful town.
And from all over, they came to our town.
They said we had very pretty girls.
And in our town, we had a-- many synagogues.
We had a Tarbut, a private school.
We had a yeshiva.
We had public schools and many, many synagogues.
And among them was-- the big synagogue where Moshe Koussevitzky used to come to our town.
It had a town that had [NON-ENGLISH].
Menachem Begin came to our town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so it was a very, very lively town and a youthful town, a lot of youth.
So that's how I can describe our town.
It had a beautiful river.
The Korchyk ran through it.
It's just really did-- the nature of the town, the way it's located, just beautiful, with a lot of greenery.
It was a beautiful, beautiful town.
Non-Jewish people lived there too, but there were-- very few were in town.
They were around the town.
In town itself-- where the majority were Jewish people lived.
Beautiful.
It was a beautiful life.
It was a carefree life.
It was-- it was a life that was-- was full with enthusiasm, with full of future, even though we weren't allowed, as Jews, to go to higher education or so on.
But we went to school.
It was really a carefree life, you know.
My father's name is Yossef and my mother's name, Chaya.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We weren't very religious but very traditional.
When it came a Shabbat, we had a Shabbat.
When it came a-- a holiday, we had a holiday like-- like it's really supposed to be.
We had kashrut in the house, yes.
That what-- it was.
But the way our family-- see, my father was a whole week, he was in the forest because he worked in there.
But when it came Friday night, he came home.
And he left on Sunday.
So it was something special when our-- when our father came home.
And I had very, very good parents, very good parents.
My mother always dwelt on education.
She always did homework with me.
And she went all over with me, you know.
The children-- there was no such a thing as making an appointment.
The children used to come.
They knocked on one another's door.
It was-- it was really nice.
And our home was-- was a very warm home.
Our parents used to come for the holidays [CRYING] from the village, from 15 kilometers from our town.
My grandparents.
Because they lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
We used to go, and they-- they used to go to the synagogue.
My father didn't go Shabbat in the synagogue, but I used to.
But when it came [NON-ENGLISH] to the special Hag ha-Shavuot [NON-ENGLISH] came for Rosh Hashannah, they came.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a very, very good childhood.
And I had a very, very-- a happy home.
Yes, I had a brother.
He was five years older than I was.
Of course--
His name is Shmuel.
And, of course, I was always wanting to tag along after them.
The boys used to chase me away.
And I used to say, as a child I used to want to tag after them, and I used to run after them, you know.
So, well-- well, it was the age difference of course.
I was-- I always wished I had someone younger than myself where I could boss them also around.
I was very much bossed around by my brother.
But, all the same, my parents stood up for me.
It was-- [CRYING] it was a happy life.
My father worked in the forest, see.
And he was-- I don't know how you call it in here.
He was an estimator.

When he looked at the-- at a tree, at any kind of certain tree, he could tell how much two-- how many two-by-fours would come out, if it's a healthy tree or if it's not a healthy-- how much wood it'll come out.
It-- it came by the meters, you know.
So he was-- and we dealt in forestry.
He was dealing with forestries because, I mean, you didn't own-- that-- that probably was leased.
And every year when-- when it was the finish of the season, you know, when they used to cut down the-- the trees, cut down the trees, they used to sell it with the sloops by the river into Poland.
Probably from there went somewhere else, I don't know.
And it was a very, very big celebration when it was the end of the season.
We had a saw mill.
My grandfather had one, but then later it was-- it burned down.
But, you know, there were people were working.
And they were cut down.
And they had a Lesnik.
That was one that looks after, you know, like-- like a forest ranger, you know, takes care of all the things.
And then it used to go they cut them down and then bring them to the river.
And it went with the river like a rafting guy.
I imagine so.
I don't know.
That that his occupation.
Oh, yes.
I always-- usually, when it came June 22 and when school was out.
So we used to go because my grandparents lived in that place, in [NON-ENGLISH] over there.
So I used to go and spend the summer holidays in there with-- my mother used to go, my brother.
We'd spend our summer holidays.
And then we went to-- we used to go into the-- we had our own-- for fish, raising fish in the forest-- carps.
So, oh yes, I was-- I was that time a child so everybody just-- I was a toy to everyone, you know.
I was the-- the center of-- of-- of the-- the family.
And the center because we had a very big office in there.
About 10 or 15 people were working in there.
So I was going from-- from one-- one person to another.
It was really-- it was beautiful in there.
We used to go to the forest and pick up blueberries and small strawberries or raspberries.
Our school was the name Tarbut, and it was called Sefer ha [NON-ENGLISH] Tarbut.
It was a private school.
It was a Hebrew school.
And in this school, we were-- we had Polish and Hebrew.
But when we learned Hebrew, we learned everything in Hebrew.
We learned Teva, we learned Hirschborn, we learned Tanakh, Torah, everything, and geography, geography, everything about Israel and so on was all in Hebrew.
Yet, we had also everything was in Polish-- like Polish history, Polish geography of the world was in Polish.
Also part, we had math also.
Even though we had it in Hebrew, we had it also in Polish.
And Shearwater-- well, it's nature.
And like in here, it's social science, I mean, or whatever.
But we-- we had separate-- zoology, biology-- that was all in Polish and then half in Hebrew.
So you see, when we went, we couldn't-- we had to speak only Hebrew.
[SPEAKING HEBREW] And we already had a United Nations at home.
My parents understood Hebrew even though they didn't speak it.
I mean, me and my brother we spoke Hebrew between ourselves.
So you know-- So it was-- and it was a very Zionistic school too.
We-- we-- we learned about that-- this one where we bought the land Keren Kayemet.
So once a month we had the our little holiday from Karen Kayemet.
And whoever collected more money [NON-ENGLISH] or sold more [NON-ENGLISH], stamps, this one was honored as a king and a queen for that-- for that month.
So we-- yeah, it was very, very Zionistic.
I mean, we grew up with that-- with that atmosphere.
And-- Did you belong as well to a youth organization?
Oh, yes.
I belonged to Gordonia because that-- they took them from a certain age, you know.
So we went to Gordonia.
When summer holidays came, of course, we were away.
But if I came for a day or two, we had the lischka, you know, a little office, we used to go down there.
We used to sing, and we used to dance.
And my father belonged to-- it was Betar, a big [INAUDIBLE].
Ze'ev Jabotinsky was the hero, you know.
And we belonged to Gordonia so actually--
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, we used to go.
But all that was done after school.
We never had it during school.
And our school, whenever it was-- I ask if you know like a--
--a party, and we had to get prepared, everything was done after school-- six o'clock, whatever.
But what-- if we-- we did dancing lessons, in the school was offered, but it's after-- after school.
We went home.
We did our homework.
We did-- and then we came back.
It was nothing was done during school.
School was strictly for education.
And the same was when we went for the-- for the Gordonia.
We-- Shabbat-- we usually met Shabbat in the afternoon.
That was the gathering.
We used to meet.
Oh, first of all we danced the hora.
We were singing all kinds of Hebrew songs.
We sang-- sung in Polish songs, you know.
There was-- we took the whole place apart.
We were so rowdy.
But, I mean, a healthy way, you know.
That what we did.
And we also had lectures, you know, on our level which they told us about Israel.
So we didn't need a map.
I could picture Israel the way from the-- from the north till the south and the east to the west.
I could see it.
You could-- you lived it.
Just lived it too.
We had a room, a separate-- you know, in like probably they rented-- we met-- it was Roberta's house.
I think they must have rented there a room because down there we used to go.
That was one room, and we used to meet there.
So every Shabbat, we used to meet.
Oh, yes.
The school was altogether in a separate building.
Yes.
The school was as a matter of fact later, was in two buildings because it couldn't accommodate us.
Was on one side of the street, and then we had to go on the other side of the street.
So, you know--
Our teachers were brought in from-- they had to finish a seminar and so on.
They were brought in.
They weren't local.
They were brought in.
Yes.
Yes.
All the teachers were Jewish teachers.
Yes, yes, yes.
We have Amana Alik-- Amana Alik, and she was Jewish, you know.
Germanic, and she was Jewish.
We had a teacher [NON-ENGLISH] was Jewish.
They-- all of them were Jewish teachers.
Yeah.
In school?
Two languages-- Polish and-- and Hebrew.
So that what we learned in school.
Hebrew.
Yeah, because otherwise if somebody would hear us different, then we wouldn't belong to [NON-ENGLISH].
It's still the picture, you know.
It's-- yeah, that's the only way.
The parents spoke Yiddish at home.
You see, my mother spoke Russian.
And my-- my-- my father with my mother, they spoke Yiddish.
And to us they spoke my mother, Russian and then sometimes in Yiddish.
My father spoke Yiddish, sometimes Polish.
But we answered in Hebrew.
Yeah.
Yiddish.
They spoke Yiddish.
Yeah, my grandparents spoke-- but my grandmother came from Russia, you know, when the Russians came.
So she didn't see her mother for 20 years.
They spoke with themselves-- between themselves Russian.
So it was like a United Nations.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes, I knew the languages.
Yeah.
Yes.
Well, because forest, it's-- it-- it-- you don't own.
It belongs to its crown land.
So it-- it's the same like in here.
When we had the business, we leased the tin barn.
If it's crown land, then you are leasing it.
No, no, no, no.
At that time, I don't-- well, I don't think so.
There must have been restrictions because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but not in this particular business.
I don't know.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Always was afraid to go-- you know, out of the town, I was afraid to go.
They used to-- to-- to scream at us, 'Yid!
Yid!' and throw stones at us.
The-- the-- the-- the children, the Ukrainians.
So we were afraid.
We were afraid of them, very much.
And the same, the Polish people used to--
So we were afraid of them.
Oh, yes.
I was always afraid to go by myself or so on.
But in town, no.
But how big was the town, you know.
And the minute you went out of town, the stones were there.
And even if somebody was passing by and they-- they came for the holidays into our town, so the-- the-- you know, into the churches, so the first thing, oh, Yid, Yid, Yid, you know.
They pointed to us, you know, like we were someone is coming out of Mars or somewhere, you know.
But yes, you can feel-- you could feel it.
I don't know.
I don't know how it's written over us.
I don't know.
And they used to say, you make fun of the sacred carrozza, you know, because the Jewry couldn't pronounce it properly and so on.
And just used to make so much fun out of us.
Certainly they would know because first of all, it's a small town.
Everybody knows everybody, you know.
Everybody knows everybody's business in a small town.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
When the war broke out, yes.
But no, it wasn't so bad.
When the war broke out in September 1, 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- our neighbors just came down, and they were kind-- their children were away in schools.
They were Polish.
Just next to us lived a Polish neighbor, Anitska.
And she-- she said to him, when the-- when the son came home, he says, oh, well, we're at war, but then-- because, you see, it was summer holidays, and he was supposed to leave.
So he said, oh, don't worry.
In about a month-- I mean, in about a week or so, well, England is going to help us, you know.
And France.
And it won't be no more war.
And everything in war will be finished and so on and so forth.

And then they were-- they-- they came out that-- that-- that we were supposed to-- they're going-- I don't know where the rumors came from because there were only maybe two or three [INAUDIBLE] in all our town-- that they're going to bomb our town.
Our town they're-- is bomb.
The-- the-- the people, you know, they-- they had the [NON-ENGLISH].
They had--
Announcements.
Yes, they had written announcements.
And by word of mouth, you know.
I mean, we are talking about a small town, you see.
Well, that-- that order from the-- from the gemina, from the city, you know.
So fine.
So that we did.
And then Mother woke us up one morning.
It took about a week or something.
And she says, you know what?
It's funny.
It looks to me-- because you see she comes from Russia herself-- she says, it looks like we went to sleep with the Polish, and-- and we-- we got up with the Russians.
The whole Russian army just came in, just-- all the Russians came in.
The Russian army came in, walked into our town.
And as they walk in, they-- they were throwing money, you know, kopekas.
You know, we [INAUDIBLE] see Russian money in the street.
I don't know.
To the people, they were throwing money in the street.
They were throwing sugar on the street.
We didn't have, you know, for-- like, for, you know, those things for about a week.
And they were giving away food, and they were very, very friendly, the Russians.
And-- but they occupied every house.
They came in.
So they-- they-- as kids, we just thought it was fun.
We didn't think much about it, you know.
So what we did, we-- they came in-- in-- into our home, and they says, now, look, you have to look after our horses.
Our soldiers are going to stay here, you know.
They just made themselves comfortable because they-- they didn't have where to stay probably, you see.
They can-- So they-- but they brought in food.
They brought all kinds of food.
So that was not-- they were very, very nice-- the Russians-- when they came in, which is wonderful to us.
Then they started with the-- school started.
And they allowed the Jewish school to be.
Yes, we had a private-- we had a Jewish school-- everything in Jewish.
They only changed, for Jewish, that instead of the long [? noun, ?] instead it had to be a short one by the end of the sentence.
That's the way they write in there-- in the-- over there.
But other ways, they-- we had [NON-ENGLISH], we had literature, you know, the Jewish literature.
And-- and also, they had Russian also.
But my mother insisted I should go to a Russian school, so I went into the Russian school.
And she was in her glory because she can communicate.
She couldn't speak Polish.
She just didn't like the Polish language.
Don't ask me why, but she just didn't like the Polish language.
She couldn't speak a word Polish.
After 20 years in the country, she couldn't speak Polish but only Russian.
So she was very, very pleased.
I mean, it was her language.
Secondly, she was-- she'll be able to get in touch with her family because her family-- she left her family in Russia.
So after a while, I mean, things started to go to normal.
We went to school, and education was A-1.
They demanded.
And I was-- then they made us from children pioneers.
We were pioneers.
They put us on with the red scarves and with-- with-- with-- with the little emblem in here.
And who says you can't brainwash.
Father Stalin-- I came home, I says he's the Father Stalin.
He's our liberator.
He's-- he's nothing but the best.
And next-- and God wasn't supposed to be mentioned, you see.
Then-- but we went to school five days a week, no six days a week.
You see we went-- we went to school on Sunday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No-- no Sunday we didn't go to school.
We had to go to school on Saturday.
And we had to write on Saturday.
So to some people was very, very hard to get used to the idea.
But when they went to the Talbut, I mean, not to-- but to the-- to the Jewish school, they had went on Sunday and didn't go Saturday.
So this was all right for them.
But--
I was-- when Russia invaded our town, I was 12, you see.
So I was finished grade six, no?
And I went into grade seven to the Russian, you know, when the Russian came.
Of course, I found it easy because my mother spoke and I knew the language.
And Ukrainian I spoke, so we learned Ukrainian and Russian.
And we did add Yiddish, but as a language, I think, once or twice a week.
We did have.
Oh, yeah, the soldiers.
Yes, soldiers coming and going all the time.
Well, we weren't very happy.
But this was not all the time.
This was only for about three, four months till it established a front, you know, on the other side of the Bug.
But then, they brought us some refugees, you see, which no country took them in.
But they ran away from the other side.
You know, like Poland is the Bug is the river.
So when they ran away from the German occupation, they're running on the Russian side.
The Russians brought-- accepted them.
They send them later on Donbass, which is in the mines, in the coal mines, and send them [INAUDIBLE] said, we did send them all over to work, which is-- I think it's legitimate.
Those, they say, thought it was crime.
It don't a crime to them.
But you see they brought them in.
And if you want to or not, this is, look, now we're bringing in 10 people, 15 people, whatever it is, you have to house the people.
And they were Polish refugees.
They were Jewish.
Jewish.
Oh, yeah, Jewish from Poland.
You know which they ran by the occupy Germany, which they managed to run away.
And they were in Russia, you see.
So they, later, they send them to Donbass, which is the-- the-- the coal mines to work.
They were treated wonderful because, I mean, at least they weren't killed, you know.
They ran.
And like in our house, every few days we had different people.
Sometimes, they'd stay for a week or two.
No.
The funniest part we didn't know.
They just-- they ran away.
The Germans were coming.
And they came.
And they ran away.
That just what we heard.
Maybe the parents knew.
You know what I mean?
But to us children-- don't forget those-- these children should have been seen but not heard.
And if they had to say something, it was whispered.
You know, it wasn't-- that we weren't allowed to hear those things.
So that what we-- but they made sure that the refugees have food.
They help-- they brought in food into the house, you know, the Russian soldiers.
So-- so it wasn't bad under the Russian regime.

Mind you, we were lucky because, you see, the rich people-- whoever was rich, whatever, you know, had businesses or so on-- they gather them and send them away in Siberia because they call them kulaki, kulak, bourgeois.
We were lucky because according to them we were working class people because my father didn't own the forestry.
He was working.
So he was considered a working person so he was continued to work for them, for the Russians, you know, in the same places that he was working.
And lucky us, they didn't send us away in Siberia.
But they nationalized my grandparents' home in [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was a big home.
It's down there where it was the-- the office, you know.
It was a very, very big home with fields and so on in the back of them there were fields.
And my-- from-- from heartbreak, my-- my-- my grandmother died.
And my grandfather went to his son to [NON-ENGLISH] and died down there from heartbreak because they took away their home, the-- the Russian.
They nationalize it, you see, so that would--
So it would change us to the good and to the bad.
You see, like, for my mother was a blessing.
You see, she had her family.
On my father's side, it was a curse.
And for us children, we had a good time.
We just didn't know any better.
We just were happy-go-lucky.

And we were singing war-- songs about the war, about Stalin, about [INAUDIBLE], and about, you know, that the-- the-- who had the first one, the Russian army will take everything away, and the [INTERPOSING VOICES] Communists will be against the best and so on and so forth.
And I just thought that's the way-- they came in school.
They told me that the man comes from a-- a-- a-- a monkey not a damsel.
You know, I mean, they brainwashed us so much that religion was put aside.
But yet in the Constitution-- when we took in school the Constitution, the Russian Constitution-- they said, you can have your religion, but we don't support your churches.
You see, you have to support that.
But it didn't mean a thing.
We were too much brainwashed as children.
You know?
Thank you.
We've finished the first tape.
